description: A local web dashboard that transforms a repository's git history into an interactive, animated force graph. Every file becomes a node, co-changed files form edges, and four complementary views (force graph, timeline scrubber, commit sidebar, overlay lenses) let developers see patterns invisible in a flat commit list.

userStories.title: Scaffold Next.js project with TypeScript, Tailwind, and test tooling
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the project scaffolded with Next.js App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v4, pnpm, and Vitest, so that all subsequent stories have a working foundation to build on.
acceptanceCriteria: pnpm install succeeds with no errors
pnpm dev starts a Next.js dev server at localhost:3000 showing a placeholder page
pnpm typecheck runs tsc --noEmit successfully
pnpm test runs Vitest and exits 0 (even with no tests yet)
pnpm lint runs ESLint with no errors
.env.example exists documenting GEMINI_API_KEY, GEMINI_MODEL, and CODEPULSE_MAX_COMMITS
react-force-graph, simple-git, @google/generative-ai, and zustand are installed as dependencies
Tailwind CSS v4 is configured and a test element with a Tailwind class renders with the correct style
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use pnpm as the package manager. Next.js App Router with TypeScript strict mode. Tailwind v4 via @tailwindcss/postcss. Vitest for unit tests. Include eslint-config-next.

userStories.title: Implement GitParser module with tests
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a GitParser module that parses a local git repository into structured RepoData, so that all git interaction is encapsulated behind a single testable interface.
acceptanceCriteria: parseRepo(repoPath, { commitLimit }) returns a RepoData object
RepoData contains an ordered commit list with hash, message, author, date, files changed, insertions, and deletions per commit
RepoData contains a file-to-commit-hashes map and a file-to-contributor-frequencies map
RepoData contains a co-change frequency map (file pair key → count) built from files co-edited in the same commit
commitLimit option caps the number of commits returned; defaults to CODEPULSE_MAX_COMMITS env var or 500
Passing a non-existent path returns a structured error object (not an unhandled exception)
Passing a path that is not a git repository returns a structured error object
Tests create a fixture git repo programmatically using simple-git, make known commits, and assert on the returned RepoData structure
Tests assert commitLimit caps the result correctly
Tests pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use simple-git. The co-change map key should be a deterministic pair string, e.g. sorted paths joined by '|'. Error shape: { ok: false, error: string }. Success shape: { ok: true, data: RepoData }.

userStories.title: Implement GraphBuilder module with tests
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a GraphBuilder module that transforms RepoData into a GraphData structure of nodes and edges, so that the force graph renderer receives clean, typed data.
acceptanceCriteria: buildGraph(repoData) returns GraphData with nodes and edges arrays
Each node has: id (file path), label (basename), commitCount, primaryContributor, contributors (author-to-count map), and firstCommitDate
Each edge has: source (file path), target (file path), coChangeCount, and lastCoChangeDate
Node count equals the number of unique files across all commits in RepoData
Edge count equals the number of unique co-changed file pairs
A file appearing in 10 commits has a higher commitCount than one appearing in 1
primaryContributor is the author with the highest frequency for that file
Unit tests use hand-crafted RepoData fixtures
Tests pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Pure function — no I/O. Node positioning is not done here; that is delegated to the force simulation at render time. firstCommitDate and lastCoChangeDate are needed by TimelineFilter.

userStories.title: Implement TimelineFilter module with tests
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a TimelineFilter module that filters GraphData to show only nodes and edges active as of a given date, so that the timeline scrubber can replay codebase history.
acceptanceCriteria: filterByDate(graphData, cutoffDate, commitsByFile) returns a filtered GraphData
Nodes whose firstCommitDate is after cutoffDate are excluded from the result
Nodes whose firstCommitDate is on or before cutoffDate are included
An edge is only included when both its source and target nodes are present in the filtered result
An edge is only included when its lastCoChangeDate is on or before the cutoff
Function is pure — same input always produces the same output with no side effects
Unit tests assert correct inclusion and exclusion across a series of cutoff dates
Tests pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This function is called on every scrubber tick so it must be fast. Avoid O(n²) algorithms. The commitsByFile map (file → sorted commit dates) can be precomputed from graphData to avoid re-scanning on every tick.

userStories.title: Implement CommitSummarizer module with tests
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a CommitSummarizer module that generates plain-English explanations of a file's commit history using Gemini, so that users can understand changes without reading raw diffs.
acceptanceCriteria: summarize(filePath, commits) calls the Gemini API and returns a plain-English narrative string
Uses @google/generative-ai SDK with GEMINI_API_KEY from environment; defaults to gemini-1.5-flash unless GEMINI_MODEL env var overrides
Only commit messages and line-change stats are sent to Gemini — no raw diff content
Commit data is chunked to stay within the model's context window when the commit list is long
Results are cached in a server-side in-memory Map keyed by filePath + lastCommitHash; a second call with the same key does not call the Gemini API again
Throws a descriptive Error (not an unhandled rejection) when the API returns an error
Returns a special AI_UNAVAILABLE sentinel value when GEMINI_API_KEY is missing or empty
Unit tests mock @google/generative-ai and assert prompting behavior, caching hit, chunking trigger, and error handling
Tests pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Server-side only module — not imported by client. The AI_UNAVAILABLE sentinel lets the API route distinguish between a real error and a missing key without throwing.

userStories.title: Implement POST /api/analyze route
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a POST /api/analyze endpoint that accepts a repo path and returns graph data, so that the client can trigger analysis without knowing how git parsing works.
acceptanceCriteria: POST /api/analyze with { repoPath, commitLimit } body returns { graphData, commitsCapped: boolean }
Returns HTTP 400 with { error: string } if repoPath is missing, does not exist, or is not a git repository
commitsCapped is true when the parsed commit count equals the commitLimit
Response contains the full GraphData produced by GitParser + GraphBuilder
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Next.js App Router route handler. Use the GitParser and GraphBuilder modules. commitLimit falls back to CODEPULSE_MAX_COMMITS env var (default 500).

userStories.title: Implement POST /api/summarize route
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a POST /api/summarize endpoint that streams a Gemini-generated commit summary, so that the sidebar can fetch AI explanations lazily.
acceptanceCriteria: POST /api/summarize with { filePath, commits } body streams a plain-text response using Next.js streaming
Returns HTTP 200 with a streaming text body when GEMINI_API_KEY is set and valid
Returns HTTP 503 with { error: 'AI_UNAVAILABLE' } when GEMINI_API_KEY is missing or empty
Delegates all summarization logic to the CommitSummarizer module
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use Next.js App Router streaming response (ReadableStream or TransformStream). Do not buffer the full Gemini response before sending.

userStories.title: Implement Zustand AppStore with full state and actions
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a Zustand store managing all application state, so that every view stays in sync without prop drilling.
acceptanceCriteria: Store contains: repoPath, graphData, filteredData, scrubberDate, selectedFile, viewMode ('default' | 'heatmap' | 'contributor'), activeContributor, searchQuery, isLoading, error, commitsCapped
loadRepo(path) calls POST /api/analyze, sets graphData and filteredData on success, sets error on failure, manages isLoading throughout
setScrubberDate(date) runs TimelineFilter client-side and updates filteredData
setSelectedFile(file | null) updates selectedFile
setViewMode(mode) updates viewMode; switching away from 'contributor' clears activeContributor
setActiveContributor(name | null) updates activeContributor
setSearchQuery(query) updates searchQuery
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: TimelineFilter is imported and run client-side on setScrubberDate to avoid a network round-trip on every scrubber tick. graphData holds the full unfiltered dataset; filteredData is the derived view.

userStories.title: Build RepoLoader landing page with path input and URL persistence
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a landing page where I can enter a local repo path and start analysis, so that I can point CodePulse at any repository on my machine.
acceptanceCriteria: The root route (/) renders a centered form with a text input for the repo path and an Analyze button
Clicking Analyze calls loadRepo on the AppStore
While isLoading is true the button shows a loading state and the input is disabled
When the store has an error a clear error message is shown below the input
The repo path is persisted to the URL as a ?repo= query parameter so it can be bookmarked
On page load, if a ?repo= query param exists the input is pre-filled with that value
Once graphData is loaded successfully the page transitions to the dashboard layout
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Apply the deep space dark theme (#0d0d1a background). This is the only page users see before analysis completes — make it feel polished and intentional, not like a placeholder.

userStories.title: Build dashboard layout shell with graph area, scrubber, sidebar slot, and header
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the dashboard layout shell with all major regions in place, so that subsequent feature stories can slot their components into a working frame.
acceptanceCriteria: Dashboard layout has a full-viewport canvas area (main), a fixed bottom bar for the scrubber, a collapsible right panel slot for the sidebar, and a top-right controls area for ViewControls
A header shows the current repo path and a Change Repo link that resets the store and returns to the RepoLoader page
When commitsCapped is true in the store a warning banner is shown indicating history is capped with the active commit limit
The graph canvas area correctly adjusts its width when the sidebar panel is open vs closed
Deep space dark background (#0d0d1a) is applied globally across all pages
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Placeholder boxes for the graph, scrubber, and sidebar slots are fine at this stage — the components will be added in subsequent stories. Focus on correct layout regions and the design theme.

userStories.title: Build ForceGraphCanvas with basic node and edge rendering
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to see all repository files as force-graph nodes with edges connecting co-changed files, so that the codebase structure becomes spatially visible.
acceptanceCriteria: ForceGraphCanvas renders a 2D canvas using react-force-graph that fills its container
All nodes from filteredData are rendered; node size is a logarithmic function of commitCount
All edges from filteredData are rendered; edge width scales linearly with coChangeCount
The force simulation runs on mount and reaches a stable layout
Clicking a node calls setSelectedFile in AppStore with the node's id (file path)
Clicking the canvas background (not a node) calls setSelectedFile(null)
Nodes present in graphData but absent from filteredData (future nodes) render at 20% opacity in gray
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use react-force-graph's 2D canvas renderer (ForceGraph2D) for performance. Pass filteredData as the graph data prop. Keep the component stateless with respect to graph data — all data flows in from the AppStore via props.

userStories.title: Add node labels, hover tooltips, and amber edge pulsing to ForceGraphCanvas
userStories.description: As a developer, I want each file node to show its name, reveal stats on hover, and have recent co-change edges glow amber, so that I can read the graph without clicking everything.
acceptanceCriteria: Each node renders its file basename as a label visible only when the node's rendered diameter on screen exceeds 8px
Hovering a node shows a tooltip with: full file path, commit count, and top contributor name
Edges whose lastCoChangeDate is within 90 days of the current scrubberDate render in amber (#f59e0b)
Edges whose lastCoChangeDate is older than 90 days before scrubberDate render in muted gray (#374151)
Default node color uses an electric blue to violet palette (e.g. #3b82f6 to #8b5cf6 based on commitCount rank)
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: react-force-graph exposes nodeLabel, nodeCanvasObject, and linkColor props for custom rendering. Use nodeCanvasObject for custom label rendering with zoom threshold logic.

userStories.title: Add node drag-to-pin and Reset Layout to ForceGraphCanvas
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to manually drag and pin file nodes to fixed positions, so that I can arrange key files for review or comparison.
acceptanceCriteria: Dragging a node pins it at the dropped position (fx and fy are set on the node data)
A pinned node does not move when the force simulation continues running
Double-clicking a pinned node unpins it (clears fx and fy) and returns it to the free simulation
A Reset Layout button in the graph toolbar clears all pinned positions and restarts the force simulation
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: react-force-graph supports node pinning via the onNodeDragEnd callback and by setting node.fx / node.fy. The Reset Layout button should mutate the node data in place and call the graph's d3ReheatSimulation method.

userStories.title: Add filename search with node highlighting and dimming
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a search input that highlights matching nodes and dims others, so that I can find a specific file quickly without losing spatial context.
acceptanceCriteria: A search input in the graph toolbar calls setSearchQuery on the AppStore on change
When searchQuery is non-empty, nodes whose file path contains the query string (case-insensitive) render at full opacity with a visible accent ring
Non-matching nodes dim to 20% opacity but remain visible and interactive
Edges connecting two non-matching nodes also dim to 20% opacity
Clearing the search input (empty string) restores all nodes and edges to their normal opacity
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Opacity logic lives in ForceGraphCanvas — it reads searchQuery from the AppStore and applies it during node/edge rendering. No changes to the underlying data are needed.

userStories.title: Build ViewControls mode toggle (Default / Heatmap / Contributor)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want three mutually exclusive view mode buttons, so that I can switch between graph coloring modes without navigating away.
acceptanceCriteria: ViewControls renders three buttons: Default, Heatmap, Contributor
Clicking a button calls setViewMode in AppStore with the corresponding value
Only one button is visually active at a time
The active button has a distinct visual state (highlighted background or border)
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: ViewControls is placed in the top-right controls area of the dashboard layout shell. It reads viewMode from AppStore to determine the active button.

userStories.title: Implement heatmap overlay coloring in ForceGraphCanvas
userStories.description: As a developer, I want nodes colored by commit frequency when heatmap mode is active, so that I can immediately see which files are most volatile.
acceptanceCriteria: When viewMode is 'heatmap', ForceGraphCanvas colors nodes on a gradient from cool blue (low commitCount) to hot red (high commitCount) relative to the max in filteredData
Heatmap coloring uses filteredData for the max value so it respects the current timeline scrubber position
A color legend is visible in the graph area showing the gradient scale with Low and High labels
Switching viewMode back to 'default' restores electric blue/violet coloring and hides the legend
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use a simple linear interpolation between two colors based on commitCount / maxCommitCount. The legend can be a small absolute-positioned div overlaid on the canvas.

userStories.title: Implement contributor lens with color assignment and click-to-isolate
userStories.description: As a developer, I want nodes colored by their primary contributor, with a legend I can click to isolate one contributor's files.
acceptanceCriteria: When viewMode is 'contributor', each node is colored by a color assigned to its primaryContributor from a palette of 10 distinct colors; contributors beyond 10 use a fallback gray
A contributor legend in the graph area lists each contributor's name with their color swatch
Clicking a contributor in the legend calls setActiveContributor in AppStore with that contributor's name; all other nodes dim to 20% opacity
Clicking the already-active contributor in the legend calls setActiveContributor(null), restoring full visibility
Switching viewMode away from 'contributor' hides the legend
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Contributor color assignment should be deterministic (sort contributors by commit count descending, assign palette colors in order) so colors are stable across renders.

userStories.title: Build TimelineScrubber with commit-snapping and date label
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a timeline scrubber at the bottom of the screen that controls which commits are visible in the graph, so that I can replay codebase history.
acceptanceCriteria: TimelineScrubber renders a full-width styled range input in the bottom bar of the dashboard
Slider range spans from index 0 (oldest commit) to index N-1 (most recent commit) where N is the commit count in graphData
The date of the commit at the current slider index is shown as a label near the slider handle
Dragging the slider calls setScrubberDate in AppStore with the date of the commit at that index (debounced at ~16ms to cap at ~60fps)
The graph updates to reflect only commits on or before the selected date
A jump-to-latest button resets the scrubber to the most recent commit index
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: The slider value is a commit index, not a timestamp, so it snaps naturally to commit positions. Map the index to a date when calling setScrubberDate.

userStories.title: Add Play, Pause, and speed controls to TimelineScrubber
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to auto-play the timeline and control its speed, so that I can watch the codebase evolve hands-free.
acceptanceCriteria: A Play button starts auto-advancing the scrubber one commit index at a time using setInterval
A Pause button (visible while playing, replacing the Play button) stops auto-advance at the current position
A speed selector offers three options: Slow (500ms per step), Normal (200ms per step), Fast (50ms per step)
Auto-play stops automatically when it reaches the most recent commit and the Play button changes to Replay
Clicking Replay resets the scrubber to the oldest commit and begins playing again
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use a React ref to hold the interval ID to avoid stale closures. Clear the interval on component unmount.

userStories.title: Build CommitSidebar with file commit history list
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a sidebar panel that slides in when I click a node and shows that file's full commit history, so that I can review its changes without leaving the graph.
acceptanceCriteria: CommitSidebar renders as a slide-in panel from the right when selectedFile is non-null in AppStore
Panel header shows the selected file's full path

Displays a chronological list of all commits that touched the selected file, each entry showing: abbreviated hash (7 chars), author name, formatted date, commit message, lines added (+), and lines removed (-)
Clicking a different node while the sidebar is open switches the panel to that file's commits without closing and reopening
A close button (×) calls setSelectedFile(null) to hide the panel
The sidebar is displayed as an overlay or slide-in that does not disable the timeline scrubber
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Commit data for the selected file is derived directly from graphData in the store — no additional API call needed for the commit list. The sidebar reads graphData.nodes to find the node, then maps its commit hashes against the commit list.

userStories.title: Add AI-generated summary section to CommitSidebar
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the sidebar to show a plain-English AI explanation of a file's recent changes, so that I can understand its history without reading raw commit messages.
acceptanceCriteria: When the sidebar opens for a file it immediately fetches from POST /api/summarize with the file path and its commits
A skeleton loader is shown in the summary area while the fetch is in progress
On success the AI-generated narrative replaces the skeleton and is scrollable if long
On error an error message and a Retry button are shown; clicking Retry re-fetches the summary
If the API returns { error: 'AI_UNAVAILABLE' } a note 'AI summaries unavailable — set GEMINI_API_KEY to enable' is shown with no error state and no retry button
The summary result is cached per file (by file path) in component or store state so revisiting the same file does not trigger a new fetch
Verify in browser using dev-browser skill
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use the Fetch API with a ReadableStream reader to display the streamed Gemini response progressively as it arrives, appending text to the summary area in real time.
